v.t: Maya civilization enters the Classic period
v.s: The Maya civilization of Mesoamerica enters the Classic period, lasting over six centuries (c. 250–900 CE). During this time, the Maya establish dozens of city-states such as Tikal, Palenque, and Copán, developing a complex hieroglyphic writing system, precise calendars (Long Count), advanced mathematics (including the concept of zero), and monumental pyramid-temples. Classic Maya civilization is one of the most brilliant pre-Columbian civilizations of the Americas.

v.t: Christianization of Armenia
v.s: King Tiridates III of Armenia, under the influence of Saint Gregory the Illuminator, establishes Christianity as the state religion, making Armenia the first Christian nation. This event not only consolidates the Armenian people but also paves the way for the legalization of Christianity in the Roman Empire, deeply influencing the history of the Caucasus region.

v.t: Sasanian Empire clashes with the Kushan Empire on the border
v.s: Under the rule of Persian Emperor Narseh, the Sasanian Dynasty launches a series of border offensives against the declining Kushan Empire to secure the eastern frontiers and control Central Asian trade routes. Sasanian forces occupy Bactria, forcing the Kushans to retreat further into the Indus Valley, thereby extending the Sasanian eastern border.

v.t: Prince of Zhao Sima Lun usurps the throne and proclaims himself emperor
v.s: During the War of the Eight Princes of the Western Jin, the Prince of Zhao, Sima Lun, stages a coup in the capital Luoyang, forcing Emperor Hui to abdicate and proclaiming himself emperor. He deposes and imprisons Emperor Hui, changing the era name to Jianshi. Sima Lun's usurpation triggers fierce opposition from other princes, including the Prince of Qi Sima Jiong and the Prince of Chengdu Sima Ying, who quickly raise armies, escalating the War of the Eight Princes.

v.t: Internal conflicts intensify within the Roman Tetrarchy
v.s: The Tetrarchy system established by Roman Emperor Diocletian falls into turmoil due to succession issues. After the forced abdication of Western Augustus Maximian, relations between his son Maxentius and Eastern Augustus Galerius deteriorate. The breakdown of trust among the emperors sets the stage for a larger civil war, ultimately leading to the collapse of the Tetrarchy.

v.t: Prince of Changsha Sima Yi kills Prince of Qi Sima Jiong
v.s: After the campaign against the Prince of Zhao, the Prince of Qi, Sima Jiong, monopolizes power in Luoyang, becoming arrogant and autocratic. The Prince of Changsha, Sima Yi, allied with the Prince of Hejian, Sima Yong, stages a coup, storming Luoyang and executing Sima Jiong. Subsequently, Sima Yi takes control of the court, but Sima Yong and the Prince of Chengdu, Sima Ying, soon unite to attack Luoyang, plunging the War of the Eight Princes into a new phase of chaos.

v.t: Diocletian issues the harshest edict against Christianity
v.s: Roman Emperor Diocletian, aiming to restore traditional polytheistic religion and strengthen imperial authority, issues the most severe persecution edict in imperial history, ordering the destruction of churches, confiscation of scriptures, prohibition of Christians from holding public office, and mandatory sacrifices. Christian leaders and believers in regions such as the Diocese of Nicomedia suffer mass executions and torture, known as the 'Great Persecution'.

v.t: Rise of Egyptian desert monasticism: formation of the tradition of Saint Anthony
v.s: In the deserts of Egypt, influenced by Saint Anthony, more and more Christians choose to withdraw from the world and become hermits. They either live in caves or form small communities, seeking union with God through prayer, asceticism, and manual labor. This movement rapidly grows, laying the foundation for the formal establishment of Christian monasticism and influencing the entire Christian world.

v.t: Zhang Chang rebels in Jiangxia
v.s: Due to the Western Jin government conscripting brave men from Jingzhou to suppress refugee uprisings in Yizhou, panic spreads among the people. Zhang Chang, a native of Jiangxia, falsely claims the court has issued orders and incites a rebellion, occupying Jiangxia Commandery. He installs a local official named Qiu Shen as the Son of Heaven and proclaims himself Prime Minister, establishing a regime. The rebel forces quickly spread across the middle Yangtze region, repeatedly defeating Jin forces and severely impacting Western Jin rule in Jingzhou.

v.t: Li Te defeated and killed in Shu, Li Xiong leads the refugees
v.s: Li Te, leader of the Di refugee movement, is ambushed and killed by the Western Jin Inspector of Yizhou, Luo Shang, in a battle north of Chengdu. His brother Li Liu and son Li Xiong gather the remnants and continue to lead the refugee uprising. Relying on outstanding military talent, Li Xiong quickly rallies forces, defeats Jin troops, and occupies the vicinity of Chengdu, laying the foundation for the later establishment of the Cheng-Han regime.

v.t: Liu Yuan proclaims himself king at Zuoguo City, establishing Han-Zhao
v.s: Liu Yuan, chieftain of the five匈奴 (Xiongnu) tribes and a descendant of Modu Chanyu, seizes the opportunity of the War of the Eight Princes and the weakness of the north to gather troops at Zuoguo City in Lishi. He proclaims himself King of Han, posthumously honors the later Shu emperor Liu Shan, and claims to restore the Han dynasty, establishing the Han-Zhao regime (historically known as Former Zhao). Liu Yuan's assumption of the title of king marks the formal beginning of the 'Sixteen Kingdoms' era.

v.t: Li Xiong proclaims himself king at Chengdu, establishing the Cheng-Han regime
v.s: After capturing Chengdu, refugee leader Li Xiong formally proclaims himself King of Chengdu, establishing the Cheng-Han regime. He abolishes Western Jin laws, reduces taxes and corvée, granting the war-torn Shu region a brief period of stability. The Cheng-Han regime holds the strategic terrain of Ba-Shu, becoming an important southern separatist power coexisting with the Eastern Jin and northern states.

v.t: Prince of Donghai Sima Yue imprisons Prince of Changsha Sima Yi
v.s: As the War of the Eight Princes reaches its climax, the Prince of Donghai, Sima Yue, conspires with imperial guards to launch a surprise attack in Luoyang, capturing and imprisoning the Prince of Changsha, Sima Yi. Sima Yue then hands him over to Zhang Fang, a general of the Prince of Hejian, who cruelly executes Sima Yi. Subsequently, Sima Yue gradually becomes the most powerful prince at court.

v.t: Buddhism becomes widespread among all social strata in China
v.s: After centuries of translation and propagation, Buddhism has transcended its early elite elite circles to penetrate deeply into Chinese folk society. The number of temples in Luoyang, Chang'an, Jianye and other cities skyrockets, and systematic Buddhist scripture translation (such as by Dharmaraksha) flourishes. Buddhist concepts of karma and reincarnation merge with indigenous Daoism and Confucianism, profoundly influencing medieval Chinese philosophy, art, and social life.

v.t: Roman Emperor Diocletian formally abdicates, beginning the end of the Tetrarchy
v.s: The Tetrarchy system established by Roman Emperor Diocletian to end the Crisis of the Third Century collapses due to succession problems. Diocletian himself and his co-emperor Maximian are forced to abdicate and retire. Their abdication triggers a new round of civil war, as Constantine, Maxentius, and others vie for the throne, plunging the Roman Empire into turmoil once again.

v.t: Western Augustus Constantius I dies in Britain
v.s: Roman Western Augustus Constantius I (father of Constantine the Great) dies at York while campaigning against the Picts in northern Britain. On his deathbed, he passes the throne to his son Constantine, who is immediately proclaimed Western Emperor by the army. This action disregards the succession rules of Diocletian's Tetrarchy, setting the stage for Constantine's eventual unification of the empire.

v.t: Prince of Donghai Sima Yue rises against Prince of Hejian Sima Yong and Prince of Chengdu Sima Ying
v.s: The Prince of Donghai, Sima Yue, allied with various prefectures in Shandong, raises an army under the pretext of escorting Emperor Hui back to Luoyang, attacking the Prince of Hejian, who controls Guanzhong, and the Prince of Chengdu, who controls Hebei. Sima Yue's forces are powerful, repeatedly defeating the coalition and gradually gaining control over Emperor Hui. The War of the Eight Princes is nearing its end, with Sima Yue emerging as the ultimate victor.

v.t: Christianity expands rapidly in the Kingdom of Aksum, growing influence of the Alexandrian diocese
v.s: The Kingdom of Aksum, located in present-day Ethiopia and Eritrea, has close ties with Roman Egypt through Red Sea trade. Influenced by Christianity from Alexandria, the Aksumite royal family and nobles begin converting to Christianity. Frumentius is consecrated as the first bishop of Aksum, and Christianity gradually becomes the state religion of Aksum, profoundly influencing East African history.

v.t: Constantine is proclaimed Western Roman Emperor by the army in Britain
v.s: After the death of his father Constantius I, Constantine rushes from Nicomedia to Britain, where he is proclaimed Western Augustus by the Roman army stationed there. He immediately marches to control Gaul, opposing Maxentius who holds Italy. Constantine's rise marks the beginning of major changes in the Roman world.

v.t: Maxentius proclaims himself emperor in Rome
v.s: Maxentius, son of former Emperor Maximian, uses discontent among the Roman Senate and Praetorian Guard over tax policies to incite a rebellion in Rome, proclaiming himself emperor. He controls Italy, Africa, and other regions, forming a three-way standoff with Eastern Emperor Galerius and Western Emperor Constantine, resulting in multiple emperors ruling simultaneously.

v.t: Emperor Hui of Jin is poisoned to death; Emperor Huai of Jin ascends the throne
v.s: After the War of the Eight Princes, the Prince of Donghai, Sima Yue, fully controls the court. He deposes and poisons the powerless Emperor Hui of Jin, then installs his brother, the Prince of Yuzhang Sima Chi, as Emperor Huai of Jin, changing the era name to Yongjia. Emperor Huai attempts to restore imperial power, but real authority remains with Sima Yue; the Western Jin's rule exists in name only.

v.t: Large-scale excavation of Buddhist grottoes begins in northern China
v.s: With the deepening spread of Buddhism in northern China, monks and believers begin carving grottoes and sculpting Buddha images on cliffs along major thoroughfares, creating spaces for meditation and worship. Early grottoes, such as the Tiantishan Grottoes in Gansu, are excavated during this period. This art form combines Indian Buddhist iconography with traditional Chinese carving techniques, initiating a thousand-year tradition of Chinese grotto temple construction.

v.t: Eastern Emperor Galerius formally recognizes Constantine as Western Augustus
v.s: To stabilize the imperial situation, Eastern Emperor Galerius reaches an agreement with Constantine at the Carnuntum conference, formally recognizing Constantine as Western Augustus. At the same time, Galerius appoints Licinius as Western Caesar. This temporary compromise averts a large-scale civil war, but tensions between East and West persist.

v.t: Sima Rui is stationed at Jianye, beginning to manage Jiangdong
v.s: The Western Jin court appoints the Prince of Langye, Sima Rui, as General of Pacifying the East and commander of military affairs for the Jiangnan region, stationing him at Jianye (later renamed Jiankang). Assisted by northern gentry like Wang Dao, Sima Rui begins to win over the southern Wu clans, reconciling north-south differences. This lays the political foundation for the later establishment of the Eastern Jin dynasty south of the Yangtze.

v.t: Wang Dao devises the strategy of 'govern with tranquility and attract all talents' for Sima Rui
v.s: Wang Dao, well aware of the resistance of southern gentry to northern refugees, personally visits and treats with courtesy leaders of the southern clans, such as Gu Rong and He Xun. He advises Sima Rui to humbly recruit southern talents, actively learn the Wu dialect, and cultivate relationships with the Wu people, gradually winning the support of the great Jiangdong families. Through this political alliance, the Jianye regime is consolidated.

v.t: Liu Yuan formally proclaims himself emperor, establishing the Han-Zhao Empire
v.s: The King of Han, Liu Yuan, formally ascends the imperial throne at Zuoguo City, adopting the state name Han (later changed to Zhao, known as Former Zhao). He posthumously honors Liu Shan as Emperor Xiaohuai and performs sacrifices to the three ancestors and five patriarchs of the Han dynasty. Liu Yuan's assumption of the imperial title signifies that the Xiongnu regime has completely broken away from the Western Jin, becoming a legitimate dynasty that rivals the Jin, drawing responses from various northern tribes.

v.t: Shi Le joins Liu Yuan, becoming an important general of Han-Zhao
v.s: Shi Le, a Jie tribesman and former slave, leads his followers from Hebei and Shandong to join Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Yuan. Liu Yuan appoints Shi Le as General of Pacifying the East, ordering him to campaign in Ji, Qing, and Yan prefectures. Relying on outstanding military talent, Shi Le rapidly rises within the Han-Zhao regime, becoming one of the most powerful military forces in the north.

v.t: Licinius is appointed Western Augustus by Eastern Emperor Galerius
v.s: To counter Constantine and Maxentius, Galerius promotes his trusted follower Licinius to Western Augustus. Licinius gains control of provinces such as Pannonia, becoming the fourth emperor in Roman politics. The power balance among the four emperors becomes increasingly fragile, with civil war imminent.

v.t: Early tribal confederation of Tubo (Tibet) begins to form
v.s: On the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, the Yarlung Valley's Yapono tribe gradually rises, with its leader considered to be Nyatri Tsenpo. Through military conquest and marriage alliances, this tribe begins to unite other tribes, forming an early slave-holding state. This is an important developmental stage of the precursor to the Tibetan Empire, laying the foundation for Songtsen Gampo's later unification of the plateau.

v.t: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Yuan's two attacks on Luoyang fail
v.s: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Yuan sends generals Liu Cong and Wang Mi south to attack Luoyang. The first attack is repelled by Western Jin forces under the Prince of Donghai, Sima Yue. Later that winter, the Han army besieges Luoyang again but retreats due to frozen rivers and lack of supplies. Although both attacks fail, they severely deplete the Jin central army, leaving Luoyang badly damaged.

v.t: Shi Le launches large-scale raids in Hebei and Shandong, establishing a base
v.s: Han-Zhao general Shi Le separates his forces from Liu Yuan's main army and campaigns north of the Yellow River. Using a strategy of 'guerrilla plunder,' he captures key cities such as Ye, Julu, and Changshan, absorbing refugees and fortified village militias. Shi Le establishes a stronghold at Xiangguo (present-day Xingtai), forming a relatively independent military bloc that later becomes the foundation for the Later Zhao.

v.t: Li Xiong formally proclaims himself emperor at Chengdu, establishing the Cheng-Han Empire
v.s: Under the urging of his subordinates, the King of Chengdu, Li Xiong, formally ascends the imperial throne with the state name Dacheng, historically known as Cheng-Han. He implements light corvée and low taxes, allowing the people to rest, bringing brief stability to the Bashu region. The Cheng-Han regime, holding strategic terrain, does not contend with northern powers and becomes one of the few southern states capable of self-preservation.

v.t: Han-Zhao founding emperor Liu Yuan dies; Liu Cong kills his brother and seizes the throne
v.s: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Yuan dies, and his crown prince Liu He succeeds. However, Liu He becomes suspicious of his brothers and tries to strip them of military power, only to be killed in a coup by his brother Liu Cong. Liu Cong then proclaims himself emperor, changing the era name to Guangxing. Known for his cruelty, he intensifies campaigns against Jin, accelerating the downfall of the Western Jin.

v.t: Roman Eastern Emperor Galerius dies, power restructured
v.s: Galerius dies of illness in Nicomedia, Asia Minor. His designated successor, Maximinus Daza, takes control of the East, while Licinius occupies European provinces. Along with Constantine and Maxentius, the Roman Empire is once again in a state of four emperors, leading to full-scale civil war.

v.t: Shi Le leads raids on the Jianghuai region, threatening Jiangnan
v.s: Shi Le is ordered to lead tens of thousands of cavalry across the Yellow River, breaking through Jin defenses and raiding the region between the Yangtze and Huai Rivers. He captures over thirty cities, advancing to Shouchun. However, due to a plague in his army, he is forced to retreat north. This southern invasion severely damages the Jianghuai economy, prompting a massive southward migration of northern gentry and commoners.

v.t: Large-scale migration of northern nomadic peoples into the Central Plains accelerates ethnic integration
v.s: The turmoil at the end of the Western Jin leads to large-scale migration of Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang, and other ethnic groups into the Central Plains. Living intermingled with the Han over long periods, they learn each other's farming and herding techniques, deepening cultural integration. Despite accompanying conflicts, this great migration lays the foundation for the grand unification and ethnic integration of the Sui-Tang period.

v.t: Disaster of Yongjia: Han-Zhao's Liu Cong captures Luoyang and takes Emperor Huai of Jin prisoner
v.s: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Cong sends generals Liu Yao, Wang Mi, and Shi Le to attack Luoyang. The Jin commander, the Prince of Donghai Sima Yue, has already died, and Jin forces collapse. Luoyang falls, Liu Yao's troops loot and burn, and Emperor Huai is captured. This event is known as the 'Disaster of Yongjia.' The central government of the Western Jin exists in name only, plunging the north into great chaos.

v.t: Sima Rui builds power in Jiangnan, forming the nucleus of a refuge regime
v.s: After the fall of Luoyang, large numbers of northern gentry flee south. Under the guidance of Wang Dao, the Prince of Langye, Sima Rui, establishes a government in Jianye, accommodating northern refugees and gentry. Although he has not yet declared himself emperor, he effectively controls areas such as Yangzhou, Jingzhou, and Jiangzhou, becoming the largest political and military bloc in the south, preparing for the establishment of the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Roman Emperor Galerius issues an Edict of Toleration on his deathbed, halting Christian persecution
v.s: While gravely ill in Nicomedia, Eastern Emperor Galerius issues an edict admitting that the persecution of Christians has failed to achieve its aims, allowing Christians to hold religious assemblies and pray for him. This is the first official legal recognition of Christianity by the Roman state, paving the way for Constantine's later Edict of Milan.

v.t: Battle of the Milvian Bridge: Constantine defeats Maxentius, unifying the West
v.s: Constantine leads his army from Gaul into Italy and meets Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge north of Rome. According to legend, Constantine sees a vision of a cross in the sky before the battle, boosting morale. Maxentius is defeated and drowns in the Tiber. Constantine becomes the sole Western Augustus and subsequently issues the Edict of Milan.

v.t: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Cong kills the captured Emperor Huai of Jin
v.s: To extinguish hopes among Jin loyalists of rallying around a southern regime, Liu Cong executes the captive Emperor Huai of Jin at Pingyang. News reaches Chang'an, where remaining Jin officials support the Prince of Qin, Sima Ye, as emperor, known as Emperor Min of Jin. The Western Jin holds only a small corner of Chang'an in the north, facing grave danger.

v.t: Shi Le occupies Xiangguo, establishing a solid base
v.s: Shi Le becomes increasingly independent within the Han-Zhao regime. He occupies Xiangguo (present-day Xingtai, Hebei) and uses it as a base to raid Youzhou and Jizhou. He recruits the Han scholar Zhang Bin as his chief strategist, implementing policies of 'broadly attracting talent and promoting agriculture,' turning Xiangguo into a powerful northern military stronghold.

v.t: Constantine meets Eastern Emperor Licinius in Milan
v.s: After defeating Maxentius, Constantine meets Eastern Emperor Licinius in Milan. They not only confirm their respective spheres of influence but also jointly issue the famous 'Edict of Milan,' declaring Christianity legal. Meanwhile, the marriage of their siblings temporarily maintains peace between the east and west.

v.t: Edict of Milan: Constantine and Licinius formally declare Christianity legal
v.s: Constantine and Licinius jointly issue an edict in Milan proclaiming freedom of religion throughout the Roman Empire, with special emphasis on Christianity. The edict orders the return of confiscated church property and recognizes Christianity as a lawful religion. This is a crucial turning point in Christianity becoming the mainstream religion of the Roman Empire and Europe.

v.t: Emperor Min of Jin Sima Ye is enthroned in Chang'an
v.s: After the death of Emperor Huai, remaining Jin ministers and generals in Guanzhong, such as Suo Lin and Qu Yun, support the Prince of Qin, Sima Ye, to ascend the throne in Chang'an as Emperor Min of Jin, changing the era name to Jianxing. However, Chang'an is dilapidated and suffering from severe famine; the court of Emperor Min essentially has only tens of thousands of troops and civilians, unable to resist Han-Zhao.

v.t: Zu Ti leads his troops north on a punitive expedition, recovering vast lands south of the Yellow River
v.s: The patriotic general Zu Ti, undeterred by the collapse of the Western Jin, leads a few hundred of his clansmen and troops north from Jingkou. Midway across the Yangtze, he strikes an oar and vows: 'If I cannot cleanse the Central Plains and return, may I be like this river!' In Yuzhou and Yanzhou, Zu Ti allies with local fortress lords, repeatedly defeating Shi Le's forces and recovering vast lands south of the Yellow River.

v.t: Former Roman Emperor Maximian commits suicide in Gaul
v.s: The abdicated former emperor Maximian, opposing Constantine's rule, flees to Marseille and attempts to proclaim himself emperor but is besieged by Constantine's forces. As the city is about to fall, Maximian is forced to commit suicide. Constantine thereby eliminates an important political rival, consolidating his rule in the West.

v.t: Construction of Jiankang (Jianye) as the political center of the Eastern Jin begins
v.s: To avoid the naming taboo of Emperor Min of Jin (Sima Ye), Jianye is renamed Jiankang. The Prince of Langye, Sima Rui, undertakes extensive construction, expanding the palace and ancestral temple, and establishing court institutions. Jiankang gradually becomes the political, economic, and cultural center of the south, laying the material foundation for the Eastern Jin to establish its capital there.

v.t: Zhang Gui and Zhang Shi establish a base in Liangzhou, forming the prototype of Former Liang
v.s: The Western Jin Inspector of Liangzhou, Zhang Gui, protects his territory and stabilizes the Hexi Corridor. After his death, his son Zhang Shi, with the Jin court having moved south and the Central Plains in turmoil, effectively becomes independent of various regimes. Although the Zhang family still nominally recognizes the Jin, it is already an independent kingdom, known historically as Former Liang. Former Liang respects Confucianism and preserves Central Plains culture.

v.t: Shi Le attacks and destroys Wang Jun in Youzhou, annexing vast areas of Jizhou
v.s: Following the strategy of his advisor Zhang Bin, Shi Le pretends to submit to the Inspector of Youzhou, Wang Jun, and sends generous gifts. Gaining Wang Jun's trust, Shi Le leads light cavalry to raid Ji City, capturing and killing Wang Jun and absorbing his followers. Thus, Shi Le occupies most of Hebei, greatly increasing his power and becoming the strongest separatist force in the north.

v.t: Church council in Asia Minor discusses the Arian controversy
v.s: As the theological dispute between the Alexandrian presbyter Arius and Bishop Alexander intensifies, a local church council is convened in Asia Minor to attempt to reconcile differences over the divinity of Christ. The council fails to reach an agreement, but sets the stage for the later First Council of Nicaea.

v.t: Tuoba Yilu, chieftain of the Tuoba Xianbei, establishes the Dai state
v.s: The Tuoba Xianbei chieftain Tuoba Yilu, for his services in assisting the Western Jin against Han-Zhao, is enfeoffed by Emperor Min of Jin as King of Dai, formally establishing the Dai state with its capital at Shengle (present-day Horinger, Inner Mongolia). This is the embryonic state of the Tuoba tribe, laying the foundation for the later Northern Wei unification of the north.

v.t: Shi Le attacks and destroys Cao Yi in Qingzhou, unifying the lower Yellow River
v.s: Shi Le sends his general Shi Hu to attack Qingzhou, defeating Cao Yi, who had proclaimed himself Governor of Qingzhou. Shi Hu captures Guanggu, and Cao Yi is killed. Shi Le thereby controls the Shandong Peninsula, forming an east-west confrontation with Liu Cong's Han-Zhao regime.

v.t: Fall of the Western Jin: Han-Zhao's Liu Yao captures Chang'an; Emperor Min surrenders
v.s: Han-Zhao general Liu Yao besieges Chang'an; food runs out and cannibalism occurs. Emperor Min of Jin is forced to surrender, and the Western Jin officially ends. Liu Yao takes Emperor Min and his officials to Pingyang. Emperor Min is humiliated and later killed by Liu Cong. The Sima family no longer has a foothold in the north.

v.t: Large-scale southward migration of northern gentry: the 'Robes and Hats Southward Migration'
v.s: After the fall of the Western Jin, Central Plains gentry and commoners, fleeing the wars, cross the Yangtze in great numbers with their entire clans. Prestigious families such as the Langye Wang, Chenjun Xie, and Qiaoguo Huan, accompanied by their dependents and retainers, flock to Sima Rui in Jiankang. The number of migrants reaches over one million. This event, known as the 'Robes and Hats Southward Migration,' promotes the economic and cultural development of Jiangnan.

v.t: Shi Le begins to establish the prototype of the Later Zhao regime
v.s: Shi Le proclaims himself King of Zhao at Xiangguo, establishing a government structure, forming the prototype of the Later Zhao regime. He has effectively broken away from Han-Zhao's Liu Cong, though not yet formally. Shi Le wins over Han gentry and reforms internal affairs, gradually strengthening Later Zhao.

v.t: Sima Rui proclaims himself Prince of Jin at Jiankang, establishing the Eastern Jin
v.s: After the fall of the Western Jin, the Prince of Langye, Sima Rui, with the support of Wang Dao, Wang Dun, and others, proclaims himself Prince of Jin at Jiankang, changing the era name to Jianwu, marking the establishment of the Eastern Jin. He formally ascends the imperial throne the following year. The Eastern Jin controls Jiangnan, Jingzhou, and Bashu, forming a north-south confrontation with the Sixteen Kingdoms in the north.

v.t: Zu Ti's great victory at Qiaocheng establishes a foothold for the Eastern Jin
v.s: Zu Ti leads his northern expedition army to a fierce battle at Qiaocheng in Yuzhou against Shi Hu's Later Zhao forces, achieving a great victory. This victory enables Zu Ti to recover vast lands north of the Huai River, and fortress lords in Yuzhou surrender one after another. The Eastern Jin court appoints Zu Ti as Inspector of Yuzhou, allowing him to gain a foothold in the north.

v.t: Liu Kun fights alone in Bingzhou, allying with Xianbei against Han-Zhao
v.s: The Western Jin Minister of Works, Liu Kun, holds Jinyang (present-day Taiyuan) in the north, isolated and alone. He allies with the Tuoba Xianbei Dai state to resist Han-Zhao. Liu Kun struggles for years, but due to internal strife in Dai and the siege of Han-Zhao, he ultimately fails to reverse the Jin's decline in the north.

v.t: Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Cong dies, causing great internal chaos
v.s: The tyrannical Han-Zhao Emperor Liu Cong dies, and his crown prince Liu Can succeeds. However, Liu Can is incompetent and is killed in a coup by his maternal relative Jin Zhun. Jin Zhun massacres the Liu imperial clan, occupies Pingyang, and declares allegiance to the Jin. Han-Zhao imperial clan members Liu Yao and Shi Le each become independent, causing the Han-Zhao Empire to split and plunging northern China into renewed chaos.

v.t: Sima Rui formally ascends the imperial throne at Jiankang, establishing the Eastern Jin
v.s: After the urging of his officials, the Prince of Jin, Sima Rui, formally ascends the imperial throne as Emperor Yuan of Jin, changing the era name to Taixing. The Eastern Jin regime is formally established. Emperor Yuan appoints Wang Dao as Grand Chancellor and Wang Dun as Grand General, forming a pattern of 'Wang and Ma sharing the empire' — a politics of aristocratic clans.

v.t: Liu Yao moves the capital to Chang'an and establishes the Former Zhao regime
v.s: After pacifying the Jin Zhun rebellion, the Han-Zhao imperial clansman Liu Yao leads his army west into Guanzhong, moves the capital to Chang'an, and ascends the imperial throne, changing the state name to Zhao, known as Former Zhao. Former Zhao controls Guanzhong and Longyou, forming an east-west confrontation with Shi Le's Later Zhao, beginning the rivalry between Former Zhao and Later Zhao during the Sixteen Kingdoms.

v.t: Shi Le proclaims himself King of Zhao at Xiangguo, establishing the Later Zhao regime
v.s: Shi Le assumes the title of King of Zhao at Xiangguo, establishing the Later Zhao regime. He appoints Zhang Bin as his chief strategist, promotes sinicization policies, establishes a Grand Academy, and perfects the official system. Under Shi Le's management, Later Zhao gradually unifies most of northern China, becoming the most powerful state in the early Sixteen Kingdoms period.

v.t: Professorships of law are established in Constantinople
v.s: Emperor Constantine, valuing legal education, orders the establishment of official professorships of law in the newly built capital, Constantinople, to teach Roman law. This promotes the systematic dissemination of Roman jurisprudence and lays the academic foundation for the later compilation of the Code of Justinian.

v.t: The Gupta Empire is founded, ushering in India's Golden Age
v.s: Chandragupta I (Moon Protector) establishes the Gupta Empire in the Magadha region, with its capital at Pataliputra. Through marriage alliances and conquest, he brings the upper Ganges valley under his control. The rise of the Gupta dynasty ends the fragmentation of northern India and ushers in the Golden Age of Indian classical civilization.

v.t: Constantine defeats Eastern Emperor Licinius, reunifying the Roman Empire
v.s: Civil war breaks out between Constantine and Eastern Augustus Licinius. Constantine twice defeats Licinius decisively at the battles of Adrianople and Chrysopolis. Licinius is forced to surrender and is later executed. Constantine becomes the sole Roman Emperor, reunifying the empire.

v.t: Zhang Mao succeeds in Former Liang, continuing the Hexi separatist regime
v.s: Zhang Shi, the founder of Former Liang, is assassinated by his subordinates; his brother Zhang Mao succeeds him. Zhang Mao continues to recognize the Jin and protect his territory. He builds the city of Guzang, constructs irrigation works, and Former Liang becomes a cultural oasis sheltering from the Central Plains wars.

v.t: Tiwanaku culture emerges in the Andean highlands of South America
v.s: On the high plateau south of Lake Titicaca, the Tiwanaku civilization enters a phase of expansion. Its unique stone architecture (such as the Gate of the Sun) and agricultural techniques (raised fields) profoundly influence the later Inca civilization. This culture becomes an important center of civilization in the Andes region.

v.t: Constantine legislates Sunday as a day of rest
v.s: Roman Emperor Constantine issues a decree declaring Sunday (the Day of the Sun) as an official day of rest, ordering all courts, workshops, and shops to close. This law incorporates Christian worship practices into imperial administration, solidifying Christianity's status in social life.

v.t: Wang Dun raises his army and enters Jiankang, beginning civil war in the Eastern Jin
v.s: Wang Dun, Grand General of the Eastern Jin, dissatisfied with Emperor Yuan's suppression of the Wang clan's power, raises an army at Wuchang under the pretext of eliminating evil ministers. He storms Jiankang, executes court officials, and sidelines Emperor Yuan. Wang Dun appoints himself Grand Chancellor and commander of all military affairs, setting a precedent for regional warlords challenging the central government in the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Shi Le essentially unifies most of northern China in Hebei
v.s: Later Zhao's Shi Le attacks and destroys the remaining Former Zhao forces in the east, defeats the Xianbei Duan tribe, and gains control of Hebei, Henan, Shandong, Shanxi, and other regions. Apart from Former Zhao holding Guanzhong, Later Zhao becomes the most powerful state in the north, laying the foundation for later unification.

v.t: Zu Ti's northern expedition fails due to court suspicion; he dies of grief and anger
v.s: After recovering the lands south of the Yellow River, Zu Ti prepares to cross north. However, Emperor Yuan, suspicious of Zu Ti's power, appoints Dai Yuan as commander in the north to restrain him. Seeing the court's lack of commitment to northern recovery and hearing of Wang Dun's rebellion, Zu Ti falls ill from grief and anger and dies at Yongqiu. The recovered territories are soon retaken by Later Zhao.

v.t: Emperor Yuan of Jin dies of grief due to Wang Dun's rebellion
v.s: Emperor Yuan of Jin, sidelined by Wang Dun and stripped of real power, falls ill from grief and humiliation and soon dies. His crown prince Sima Shao succeeds as Emperor Ming of Jin. Wang Dun had hoped to usurp the throne, but fearing opposition from other aristocratic clans, he temporarily withdraws to Wuchang.

v.t: Constantine defeats the Danubian Goths, stabilizing the northern border
v.s: Constantine personally leads a campaign against the Gothic tribes on the lower Danube, achieving a great victory. He forces the Goths to renew the peace treaty, return Roman captives, and allows them to settle as allies on the Roman border. This campaign secures the northern frontier, removing a threat to the later eastern campaigns.

v.t: The Eastern Jin court puts down Wang Dun's rebellion
v.s: Wang Dun again raises troops to rebel, but falls seriously ill en route, leaving command to his subordinates. Emperor Ming of Jin, with the strategies of Wen Qiao, Yu Liang, and others, organizes a counterattack that decisively defeats Wang Dun's rebel forces. Although Wang Dun dies, his remaining followers are eliminated. The Eastern Jin's central authority is briefly restored.

v.t: Constantine again defeats the remnants of Licinius, achieving complete unification
v.s: After defeating Licinius, Constantine wipes out the last remnants of his supporters in the Black Sea region, consolidating his rule over the entire empire. He then issues a series of decrees reshaping the imperial administration and military structure, preparing for the transfer of the capital to Constantinople.

v.t: Emperor Constantine the Great decides to build a new capital on the site of Byzantium, naming it Constantinople
v.s: Emperor Constantine the Great selects the ancient Greek city of Byzantium as the new capital of the Roman Empire. He personally measures the land and plans the walls, palaces, hippodrome, and churches; large-scale construction begins immediately. The new capital will combine the strategic advantage of being a European-Asian transportation hub, symbolizing the eastward shift of the empire's center of gravity.

v.t: First Council of Nicaea convenes, establishing basic Christian doctrine
v.s: Emperor Constantine convenes the first ecumenical council of Christianity at Nicaea in Asia Minor, attended by about three hundred bishops. The council strongly condemns the Arian view denying the full divinity of Christ, formulates the Nicene Creed, and establishes the orthodox doctrine of the 'consubstantiality' of the Father and the Son. It also fixes the date of Easter.

v.t: Former Zhao's Liu Yao attacks the Former Liang regime in Liangzhou
v.s: Former Zhao Emperor Liu Yao sends troops to attack the Longxi region under Former Liang's control, but is defeated. Zhang Jun of Former Liang pursues and recovers some lost territory. This battle preserves Former Liang's independence in the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Jin dies; Emperor Cheng of Jin ascends as a child
v.s: Emperor Ming of Jin dies of illness, and his five-year-old crown prince Sima Yan ascends as Emperor Cheng of Jin. Due to his youth, Empress Dowager Yu (mother) rules as regent, assisted by Yu Liang, Wang Dao, and others. Yu Liang's autocratic rule later leads to the Su-Jun Rebellion.

v.t: Emperor Constantine the Great executes his eldest son Crispus
v.s: Crispus, the eldest son of Constantine the Great (a battle-hardened general), is accused of conspiracy by his stepmother Fausta and sentenced to death. Soon, Constantine discovers the truth and executes Fausta as well. This palace tragedy fundamentally changes the imperial succession.

v.t: Yu Liang's autocratic rule intensifies internal conflicts in the Eastern Jin
v.s: After Emperor Cheng's accession, the imperial in-law Yu Liang monopolizes court affairs, excluding older ministers like Wang Dao. He orders the governor of Lili, Su Jun, to surrender his military command, provoking Su Jun and Zu Yue to join forces in rebellion. The conflict between the central government and regional powers escalates.

v.t: Su Jun and Zu Yue rebellion: Su Jun captures Jiankang
v.s: The governor of Lili, Su Jun, together with the Inspector of Yuzhou, Zu Yue, rebels under the pretext of 'clearing the ruler's side' and attacks Jiankang. Yu Liang's imperial guard is routed, and Su Jun's forces enter Jiankang, looting and burning the palaces. Yu Liang flees, and Emperor Cheng is taken hostage.

v.t: Shi Le unifies the north; Later Zhao reaches its peak
v.s: Later Zhao's Shi Le attacks and captures Former Zhao Emperor Liu Yao, completely destroying Former Zhao and unifying northern China (except for Former Liang and Dai). Shi Le becomes the first emperor of the Sixteen Kingdoms to unify the north; Later Zhao's territory stretches from the sea in the east to Longyou in the west, to the Great Wall in the north and the Huai River in the south.

v.t: Later Zhao's Shi Hu defeats Former Zhao's Liu Yao, destroying Former Zhao
v.s: Later Zhao Emperor Shi Le sends his general Shi Hu to fight Former Zhao's Liu Yao in a decisive battle at Luoyang. Shi Hu leads from the front, routing the Former Zhao army and capturing Liu Yao alive. Later Zhao then takes Chang'an, and Former Zhao falls. Shi Le completes the basic unification of northern China.

v.t: Tao Kan and Wen Qiao put down the Su Jun rebellion
v.s: The Inspector of Jingzhou, Tao Kan, together with the Inspector of Jiangzhou, Wen Qiao, unite the king's forces to attack Su Jun. After fierce fighting, the coalition kills Su Jun and recovers Jiankang. Tao Kan is promoted to Grand Tutor, commanding eight provinces, becoming the most powerful regional warlord in the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Construction begins on the great defensive walls of Constantinople
v.s: To protect the new capital, Emperor Constantine the Great orders the construction of defensive walls from the Golden Horn to the Sea of Marmara. These walls, later reinforced many times, become one of the most formidable fortification systems of the Middle Ages, protecting Constantinople for a thousand years.

v.t: Later Zhao's Shi Le formally proclaims himself emperor and enfeoffs his ministers
v.s: After unifying the north, Shi Le formally ascends the imperial throne, still with the state name Zhao (Later Zhao). He posthumously honors his ancestors and enfeoffs imperial clan members and meritorious officials. Although of slave origin, Shi Le is receptive to advice and promotes sinicization policies, making Later Zhao the most powerful state in East Asia at the time.

v.t: Emperor Constantine the Great formally moves the capital to Constantinople
v.s: Constantine the Great officially moves the Roman capital from Rome to the newly built Constantinople (formerly Byzantium). The new capital, called 'Nova Roma,' has a Senate and a complete administrative structure. This move shifts the empire's center of gravity eastward to the culturally and commercially advanced Greek-speaking regions.

v.t: Constantinople is formally inaugurated
v.s: Roman Emperor Constantine the Great holds a grand inauguration ceremony for the newly built capital. The city is laid out entirely in the Roman manner: seven hills, a Senate, forums, and a hippodrome, and is officially named 'Nova Roma.' Controlling the Bosporus Strait, Constantinople becomes a hub of Eurasian trade and remains the heart of the Eastern Roman Empire for over a thousand years.

v.t: The Hippodrome of Constantinople begins hosting chariot races
v.s: Inaugurated together with the capital, the Hippodrome of Constantinople can hold tens of thousands of spectators and holds its first grand chariot races. Thereafter, the hippodrome becomes the center of political and entertainment life for the citizens of Constantinople, witnessing major events such as the Nika riots.

v.t: Eastern Jin's northern expeditions: remnants of Zu Ti's forces continue activities in the Huai River region
v.s: Although Zu Ti is dead, his brother Zu Yue inherits part of his army and continues to hold positions north of the Huai. The Eastern Jin court also organizes several small-scale northern expeditions, but none achieve significant progress due to internal power struggles. Zu Yue later joins the Su Jun rebellion, and the northern expedition forces completely disintegrate.

v.t: Later Zhao's Shi Le establishes a Grand Academy, promoting Confucian education
v.s: Later Zhao Emperor Shi Le, who respects Confucianism and loves learning, establishes a Grand Academy at Xiangguo, ordering the children of gentry to study the Confucian classics. He also revives systems of selecting filial and incorruptible officials, bringing Han gentry into government and promoting the sinicization of northern ethnic groups.

v.t: Emperor Constantine issues laws encouraging Christianization
v.s: Emperor Constantine issues a series of laws encouraging imperial officials and the populace to convert to Christianity. He grants churches the right of inheritance, gives bishops judicial authority, and exempts clergy from municipal duties. Christianity's status in social institutions rapidly rises.

v.t: The distinguished official Tao Kan, with great power, is increasingly suspected in his later years
v.s: Tao Kan, Grand Tutor of the Eastern Jin, after putting down the Su Jun rebellion, commands military affairs over eight provinces, controlling a large army. The court both relies on him and suspects him. In his later years, Tao Kan repeatedly requests to retire to his hometown, but is refused, and ultimately dies of anxiety.

v.t: Later Zhao founding emperor Shi Le dies; Shi Hu usurps power
v.s: Later Zhao Emperor Shi Le dies, and his crown prince Shi Hong succeeds. However, Shi Le's nephew Shi Hu, who controls the army, stages a coup, deposing Shi Hong and proclaiming himself Heavenly King. Shi Hu is cruel and bloodthirsty, plunging Later Zhao into a dark period of tyranny and intensifying social conflicts.

v.t: Eastern Jin minister Tao Kan dies
v.s: Tao Kan, Grand Tutor and Duke of Changsha of the Eastern Jin, dies in Jiankang. During his tenure, he was diligent in government, frugal and devoted to the public good, deeply loved by the people. His death deprives the Eastern Jin of a statesman capable of stabilizing Jingzhou, and court affairs fall into the hands of Yu Liang and Yu Yi.

v.t: Sasanian Persia and the Roman Empire clash in Mesopotamia
v.s: Sasanian Persian Emperor Shapur II takes advantage of the Roman Empire's internal adjustments after the capital relocation to harass the Roman eastern frontier, vying for control of Mesopotamia. Roman defenders fight tenaciously, with both sides suffering victories and losses, setting the stage for later major wars.

v.t: Cheng-Han founding emperor Li Xiong dies; internal strife begins in Cheng-Han
v.s: Cheng-Han Emperor Li Xiong dies; his nephew Li Ban, son of his brother Li Dang, succeeds. However, Li Xiong's sons Li Yue and Li Qi stage a coup, killing Li Ban, and Li Qi proclaims himself emperor. The Cheng-Han imperial family turns against each other, and the state's power rapidly declines.

v.t: Shi Hu's tyrannical rule over Later Zhao; he massacres imperial clan members and ministers
v.s: Shi Hu, the cruel and tyrannical Heavenly King of Later Zhao, massacres many of Shi Le's former subordinates and imperial clan members. He executes his own crown prince Shi Sui, favors eunuchs and sorcerers, and lives in extravagance, building grand palaces. Later Zhao's foundations are shaken by Shi Hu's tyranny.

v.t: Constantine strengthens the Danubian frontier
v.s: Emperor Constantine inspects the Danubian border, reinforcing fortresses and ramparts to guard against barbarian invasions by Goths, Sarmatians, and others. He also allows some barbarians to settle as allies within the empire to defend the frontier, adopting a strategy of using barbarians to counter barbarians.

v.t: Constantine the Great partitions the empire among his sons as future heirs
v.s: Constantine the Great divides the empire among his three sons: Constantine II, Constantius II, and Constans I, who will rule Gaul, the East, Italy, and other regions respectively. He also establishes the office of praetorian prefect, laying the institutional groundwork for the future division of the empire.

v.t: Later Zhao's Shi Hu massively constructs palaces in Yecheng, exhausting the people
v.s: Shi Hu conscripts hundreds of thousands of laborers to build luxurious palaces, gardens, and terraces in Yecheng. He also builds a hunting park west of Yecheng, forcing people to relocate from their ancestral lands. The heavy corvée makes life unbearable for the northern people.

v.t: The Eastern Jin implements the 'Tuduan' policy, reorganizing household registration
v.s: To increase tax revenue and military conscription, the Eastern Jin court implements the 'Tuduan' policy in Jiangnan, canceling the 'Qiao' commanderies and counties (established for northern refugees) and incorporating the refugee population into local household registers, making them liable for corvée and taxes like the natives. This policy strengthens central control but also intensifies conflicts between northern and southern gentry.

v.t: Continued controversy over Arian Christianity within the Eastern Empire
v.s: Although the Council of Nicaea condemned Arianism, it still has influence in the Eastern Empire. Emperor Constantine the Great, influenced by Arianism in his later years, begins to tolerate it, leading to intensifying internal Christian strife and deepening the potential for schism.

v.t: Roman Emperor Constantine the Great dies
v.s: Constantine the Great dies in his palace near Nicomedia, receiving baptism as a Christian on his deathbed. During his life, he reunified the Roman Empire, moved the capital to Constantinople, legalized Christianity, and convened the Council of Nicaea, profoundly changing the course of world history.

v.t: Murong Huang proclaims himself King of Yan, establishing the Former Yan regime
v.s: The chieftain of the Murong Xianbei tribe, Murong Huang, proclaims himself King of Yan at Changli, establishing the Former Yan regime. He employs Han gentry, establishes a bureaucracy modeled on the Jin, and implements a mixed economy of farming and pastoralism. Former Yan gradually expands into western Liaoning and eastern Hebei, becoming a major power during the Sixteen Kingdoms.

v.t: King Ezana of Aksum begins his reign; the kingdom flourishes
v.s: King Ezana ascends to the throne of Aksum. During his reign, he conquers the Kingdom of Kush, controls Red Sea trade, and formally makes Christianity the state religion. Aksum becomes the most powerful state in the Horn of Africa, ranking alongside Rome and Persia as one of the three great powers of the time.

v.t: Constantine II attacks his brother Constans, is defeated and killed
v.s: Constantine II, eldest son of Constantine the Great, resenting the allocation of territories, invades Italy, which is ruled by his younger brother Constans I. During the attack, Constantine II falls into an ambush and is killed. Constans then takes over his brother's territory, becoming the sole ruler of the Western Empire.

v.t: Tuoba Shiyijian ascends the throne of Dai, re-establishing the Dai state
v.s: The Tuoba Xianbei chieftain Tuoba Shiyijian ascends the throne of Dai at Fanqi, re-establishing the Dai state, which had declined due to internal strife. He formulates laws, establishes a bureaucracy, and sets his capital at Shengle, enabling the Tuoba tribe to rise rapidly and lay the foundation for the later Northern Wei unification of the north.

v.t: Later Zhao's Shi Hu attacks Former Yan, suffers heavy losses
v.s: Shi Hu leads a large army to attack Former Yan's Murong Huang, but Murong Huang defends strategically. With supply difficulties and heavy snowfall, Shi Hu is forced to withdraw, and is ambushed on the retreat by Former Yan cavalry, suffering a major defeat. Former Yan successfully stops Later Zhao's expansion.

v.t: Constantius II is proclaimed Eastern Emperor
v.s: After Constantine the Great's death, his son Constantius II is proclaimed Augustus in the East. He then defeats a usurper, consolidates control over the East, and co-rules the Roman Empire with his brother Constans I.

v.t: Yu Liang's northern expedition against Later Zhao fails
v.s: The Eastern Jin General Who Conquers the West, Yu Liang, launches a northern expedition, attacking Later Zhao's Xiangyang. Due to poor preparation and discord among the generals, the Jin army is routed, suffering heavy losses. This failure severely demoralizes the Eastern Jin's northern expedition efforts and damages Yu Liang's prestige.

v.t: Eastern Jin Grand Chancellor Wang Dao dies
v.s: Wang Dao, Grand Chancellor and Duke of Shixing of the Eastern Jin, dies in Jiankang. He was a founder of the Eastern Jin regime, serving Emperors Yuan, Ming, and Cheng, advocating 'govern with tranquility and rule with benevolence,' and maintaining the partial peace south of the Yangtze. After his death, the court becomes increasingly chaotic.

v.t: The Sasanian Persian Empire begins a large-scale persecution of Christians
v.s: Due to the growing influence of Christianity in Armenia and the Roman Empire, and suspicion that Christians are pro-Roman, Sasanian Emperor Shapur II orders a nationwide persecution of Christians, confiscating churches and property. Many Persian Christians are martyred, while some flee to Roman territory.

v.t: Sasanian art reaches a golden age
v.s: Under Shapur II, the Sasanian Empire achieves brilliant accomplishments in metalwork, relief sculpture, and architecture. Court silver plates depict scenes of the king hunting and feasting, and large rock reliefs at Naqsh-e Rostam record victories over Rome, profoundly influencing Byzantine and Islamic art.

v.t: The Eastern Jin court reorganizes the Qiao commanderies and counties
v.s: The Eastern Jin further implements the 'Tuduan' policy, reorganizing the Qiao commanderies and counties, and reducing the atmosphere of vanity and hypocrisy. The court attempts to establish a clearer household registration and tax system, but faces resistance from the great Qiao clans, with limited results.

v.t: Gothic bishop Ulfilas promotes the Christianization of the Goths
v.s: To facilitate missionary work, the Gothic bishop Ulfilas invents the Gothic alphabet and translates the Bible into Gothic. He also leads a portion of the Goths to migrate into Roman territory, promoting the Christianization of the Germanic barbarians. Arian Christianity becomes widespread among the Goths.

v.t: Constantius II launches a war against Persia
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II personally leads a large army east against the Sasanian Persian Empire, vying for control of Mesopotamia and Armenia. The two sides clash fiercely at Nisibis and elsewhere, with mixed results. The war continues for several years, draining the resources of both empires.

v.t: Emperor Cheng of Jin dies; Emperor Kang of Jin succeeds
v.s: Emperor Cheng of Jin dies. As his son is too young, his brother Sima Yue ascends as Emperor Kang of Jin. Emperor Kang's reign is short, and power is dominated by the Yu clan of imperial in-laws.

v.t: Yu Yi of the Eastern Jin actively prepares for a northern expedition
v.s: The Eastern Jin General Who Pacifies the West, Yu Yi, aiming to fulfill his brother Yu Liang's ambition, reorganizes the military in Jingzhou and trains troops. He sends envoys to contact Former Yan and Former Liang, attempting to coordinate a northern expedition against Later Zhao. However, due to resistance at court, the expedition never takes place.

v.t: The Nicene Creed is reaffirmed as authoritative in the Eastern Empire
v.s: Under pressure from orthodox factions, Constantius II convokes a church council to reaffirm the authority of the Nicene Creed and exile Arian bishops. However, because the emperor himself leans toward Arianism, the religious strife does not subside.

v.t: Emperor Kang of Jin dies; his young son Emperor Mu of Jin succeeds
v.s: Emperor Kang of Jin dies, and his two-year-old crown prince Sima Dan ascends as Emperor Mu of Jin. His mother, Empress Dowager Chu, serves as regent, assisted by the Prince of Kuaiji, Sima Yu. The Eastern Jin court becomes even more chaotic, with powerful officials vying for power.

v.t: Long-term confrontation between the Eastern Jin and northern regimes along the Huai River line
v.s: Later Zhao is powerful, and the Eastern Jin uses the Huai River as its border. The two sides battle for years. The Eastern Jin establishes Qiao commanderies north of the Yangtze to settle southern refugees, organizes agricultural colonies, and forms a long-standing pattern of north-south confrontation.

v.t: Wang Xizhi serves as Grand Administrator of Kuaiji, his calligraphy reaches its peak
v.s: Wang Xizhi, the Eastern Jin's Sage of Calligraphy, serves as Grand Administrator of Kuaiji, socializing with famous scholars at Lanting and elsewhere. His calligraphy integrates ancient and modern styles, creating a new natural and free-spirited style. His representative work from this period, the 'Preface to the Lanting Collection,' is hailed as the 'best running script under heaven,' marking an era of self-consciousness in Chinese calligraphy.

v.t: Huan Wen becomes Inspector of Jingzhou, gaining control of the upper Yangtze army
v.s: The Eastern Jin court appoints Huan Wen as General of Pacifying the West and Inspector of Jingzhou, commanding military affairs over six provinces including Jing and Liang. Huan Wen, stationed at Jiangling, controls the elite troops of the upper Yangtze, gradually becoming the most powerful minister in the Eastern Jin, laying the groundwork for later northern expeditions and usurpation attempts.

v.t: Shapur II of Sasanian Persia captures Roman fortresses in Mesopotamia
v.s: Shapur II leads a large army to capture the Roman border fortress of Singara (in present-day Iraq), where the Roman garrison is annihilated. The Persian army subsequently storms Amida and other fortresses, forcing the Roman Empire to sue for peace, ransoming captives for a huge sum of gold.

v.t: Huan Wen attacks Shu, destroying the Cheng-Han regime
v.s: The Inspector of Jingzhou, Huan Wen, leads his army up the Yangtze River to attack Cheng-Han. The Cheng-Han emperor Li Shi is inept and unprepared; Huan Wen reaches the gates of Chengdu, Li Shi surrenders, and Cheng-Han is destroyed. The Eastern Jin reunifies Bashu, greatly enhancing its national strength.

v.t: The Roman Empire reorganizes its eastern defensive system to counter the Persian threat
v.s: After suffering heavy losses in the Persian war, Roman Emperor Constantius II restructures the eastern border defenses, strengthening fortresses such as Nisibis and adopting a new tactic combining mobile field armies with border garrison troops.

v.t: The Eastern Jin fully controls Bashu, establishing Yizhou
v.s: After Huan Wen pacifies Cheng-Han, the Eastern Jin reincorporates Bashu into its administrative system, appointing officials to manage the region. The rich resources and strategic terrain of Shu provide the Eastern Jin with a strategic buffer and source of supplies.

v.t: Pu Hong, chieftain of the Di tribe, leads his people to migrate to Guanzhong
v.s: Taking advantage of the internal turmoil in Later Zhao, the Di chieftain Pu Hong leads his followers from Longxi to eastern Guanzhong, building up strength for the future establishment of the Former Qin regime. Pu Hong is later poisoned by a Later Zhao general, and his son Pu Jian succeeds him.

v.t: Murong Huang of Former Yan dies; Murong Jun succeeds
v.s: The Wenming Emperor of Former Yan, Murong Huang, dies; his son Murong Jun succeeds. Murong Jun continues his father's ambition to expand territory, employing Han gentry, and Former Yan's power continues to grow.

v.t: Hinduism and Buddhism flourish together in the Gupta Empire
v.s: Under the patronage of the Gupta Empire, both Hinduism (especially Vaishnavism and Shaivism) and Buddhism prosper. Chinese monks like Faxian travel west to India, bringing back numerous Buddhist scriptures. Nalanda University begins to take shape as a center of Buddhist learning.

v.t: Later Zhao tyrant Shi Hu dies; succession struggle and internal chaos erupt
v.s: Later Zhao Emperor Shi Hu dies, and his crown prince Shi Shi succeeds. However, Shi Hu's sons Shi Zun, Shi Jian, and Shi Bao fight among themselves for the throne. The Later Zhao court descends into unprecedented chaos, and Ran Min rises to prominence.

v.t: Ran Min emerges during the Later Zhao civil war, gaining military control
v.s: During the Later Zhao civil war, the general Ran Min (adopted grandson of Shi Hu) kills Shi Zun, installs Shi Jian as emperor, and appoints himself Grand General, taking control of the government. He then kills Shi Jian and prepares to proclaim himself emperor. Ran Min's Han identity triggers ethnic reprisals.

v.t: The Roman Empire recruits large numbers of Germanic mercenaries to strengthen border defenses
v.s: Due to the heavy losses in the war with Persia, Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II vigorously recruits Germanic barbarians (Franks, Alemanni, etc.) into the army, even appointing barbarian generals to high office. This temporarily solves manpower problems but sows the seeds for future barbarian control of the Roman army.

v.t: Ran Min issues the 'Kill the Barbarians' order and proclaims himself emperor, establishing the Ran Wei regime
v.s: After seizing power from Later Zhao, Ran Min issues the 'Kill the Barbarians' order, calling on Han people to massacre Hu barbarians, and proclaims himself emperor with the state name Da Wei, known as Ran Wei. He massacres a large number of Jie and Xiongnu people, plunging the former Later Zhao territories into unprecedented ethnic slaughter.

v.t: Pu Jian establishes the Former Qin regime, making Chang'an its capital
v.s: The Di chieftain Pu Jian leads his followers into Guanzhong, occupies Chang'an, and proclaims himself Heavenly King, establishing the Former Qin regime. Based in Guanzhong, Former Qin promotes sinicization policies and quickly rises as a powerful northern state, replacing Later Zhao.

v.t: Magnentius proclaims himself emperor in the Western Roman Empire
v.s: The Western Roman general Magnentius stages a coup in Gaul, killing Emperor Constans I and proclaiming himself Western Augustus. Constantius II, upon hearing the news, raises an army to march west, plunging the Roman Empire into civil war again.

v.t: The Gupta Empire unifies most of northern India
v.s: Samudragupta (Sea Protector), the second monarch of the Gupta dynasty, through extensive conquests, expands the Gupta Empire from the upper Ganges to the eastern Deccan Plateau, forcing peripheral states to pay tribute, essentially unifying northern India.

v.t: The Maya civilization perfects its complex calendrical system
v.s: Maya astronomers in Mesoamerica, through long-term observations, perfect a calendrical system based on the 365-day Haab' and the 260-day Tzolk'in, and create the Long Count calendar, which calculates time spans exceeding ten million years with precision.

v.t: Former Qin's Pu Jian formally proclaims himself emperor
v.s: The Former Qin Heavenly King Pu Jian formally ascends the imperial throne at Chang'an, with the state name Qin. He models his government on Han institutions, establishes a bureaucracy, and promotes agriculture, further consolidating the Former Qin regime. In the same year, he defeats an Eastern Jin northern expedition force, consolidating control over Guanzhong.

v.t: Ran Min engages Murong Jun in battle; Ran Wei's strength is severely depleted
v.s: Former Yan's Murong Jun takes advantage of the civil war in Later Zhao and the nascent state of Ran Wei to march south and attack Ran Min. The two sides fight fiercely at Lian Tai; although Ran Min is brave, he is outnumbered and defeated by the Former Yan army, and the main force of Ran Wei is virtually destroyed.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II defeats the usurper Magnentius
v.s: Constantius II engages Magnentius in a decisive battle in the Danube region and achieves a great victory. Magnentius flees back to Gaul and commits suicide; Constantius II reunifies the Roman Empire.

v.t: Former Yan's Murong Jun destroys Ran Wei, unifying Hebei
v.s: The Former Yan army storms the Ran Wei capital Yecheng, captures and executes Ran Min. Former Yan annexes the former Ran Wei territories, unifying the Hebei region and becoming one of the most powerful regimes in the north. Murong Jun proclaims himself emperor and moves the capital to Yecheng.

v.t: Constantius II appoints his cousin Julian as Western Caesar
v.s: To strengthen the defense of the west, Constantius II appoints his cousin Julian as Western Caesar, responsible for governing Gaul and Britain and defending against Germanic barbarian incursions. Julian thereby enters the historical stage.

v.t: Lanting Gathering: Wang Xizhi creates the 'Preface to the Lanting Collection'
v.s: In early spring, Wang Xizhi, the Eastern Jin's Sage of Calligraphy, together with Xie An, Sun Chuo, and more than forty other famous scholars, holds a purification gathering at Lanting in Kuaiji Shanyin. The participants engage in the 'winding stream' drinking game and compose poetry, and Wang Xizhi writes a preface to the poetry collection, the 'Preface to the Lanting Collection,' known as the finest running script under heaven. This work epitomizes the Wei-Jin demeanor and the age of self-conscious calligraphy.

v.t: Constantius II becomes the sole emperor of the Roman Empire
v.s: After defeating Magnentius and suppressing other usurpers, Constantius II becomes the sole Roman emperor. He continues to promote Arian Christianity and suppresses the orthodox faction, leading to further schism between the eastern and western churches.

v.t: Huan Wen's first northern expedition against Former Qin reaches the Ba River
v.s: Huan Wen, the powerful minister of the Eastern Jin, leads 40,000 infantry and cavalry from Jiangling north to attack Former Qin. The Jin army advances swiftly, reaching the Ba River (eastern suburbs of Chang'an), and the people of Guanzhong respond enthusiastically. However, due to insufficient supplies, Huan Wen is forced to retreat, and the expedition fails just as it seemed successful.

v.t: Western Caesar Julian resists Germanic invasions in Gaul
v.s: Julian leads Roman legions in Gaul, repeatedly defeating invading Franks and Alemanni. He repairs roads, rebuilds cities, restores order, wins the hearts of soldiers and civilians, and accumulates capital for his later bid for the imperial throne.

v.t: Former Qin founding emperor Pu Jian dies; the tyrant Fu Sheng succeeds
v.s: Former Qin Emperor Pu Jian dies, and his crown prince Fu Sheng ascends. Fu Sheng is cruel and unpredictable, killing ministers and imperial clan members at will, plunging Former Qin into political chaos and panic. Fu Jian and Wang Meng begin to plot a coup.

v.t: The Alemanni invade Gaul on a large scale; Julian responds
v.s: The Germanic Alemanni tribal confederation, taking advantage of the Roman civil war, launches a large-scale crossing of the Rhine, pillaging the interior of Gaul. Julian organizes Roman legions and auxiliary forces, massing near Strasbourg for a decisive battle.

v.t: Battle of Strasbourg: Julian decisively defeats the Alemanni
v.s: Julian leads 13,000 Roman troops against 30,000 Alemanni at Strasbourg. Relying on tight infantry formations and elite cavalry, the Roman legions rout the barbarians, killing their chieftain and nearly annihilating the enemy force. This victory restores long-term peace to the Rhine frontier.

v.t: Huan Wen's second northern expedition recovers Luoyang
v.s: Huan Wen leads an army north to attack Yao Xiang, who has defected from the Jin to Former Yan. He defeats Yao Xiang decisively at the Yi River. Yao Xiang flees to Guanzhong, and Huan Wen advances to occupy Luoyang. The Eastern Jin court appoints Huan Wen as Grand Commander of the Conquest, but Luoyang is soon retaken by Former Yan.

v.t: Fu Jian kills Fu Sheng, proclaims himself emperor of Former Qin, with Wang Meng as chief minister
v.s: Fu Jian, Prince of Donghai of Former Qin, together with his strategist Wang Meng, stages a coup, deposing and killing the tyrant Fu Sheng, and proclaims himself Heavenly King of Great Qin. Fu Jian places Wang Meng in charge of the government, implementing reforms, and Former Qin embarks on a path to prosperity and power.

v.t: Julian rebuilds cities and restores order in Gaul
v.s: In Gaul, Julian not only conducts military defense but also devotes himself to political reconstruction. He reduces taxes, restores city councils, and repairs Gaulish urban infrastructure, reviving the war-ravaged region and earning the deep affection of the Gaulish people.

v.t: Wang Meng is appointed Palace Attendant and Head of the Secretariat in Former Qin, promoting legal reforms
v.s: Wang Meng is appointed Palace Attendant and Head of the Secretariat by Fu Jian, taking charge of confidential affairs. He strictly enforces the law, combats powerful families, reorganizes the bureaucracy, and strengthens central control, making Former Qin politically clear, financially sound, and militarily strong.

v.t: Wang Meng directs civil service reform in Former Qin, dismissing hundreds of incompetent officials
v.s: With Fu Jian's support, Wang Meng vigorously reorganizes the Former Qin bureaucracy. He sends inspectors to the provinces to investigate corruption and malfeasance, dismissing over four hundred incompetent officials in one sweep and promoting honest and talented individuals. The administrative efficiency of Former Qin greatly improves.

v.t: Julian is recalled by the emperor to Gaul, stationed in Paris to face the barbarians
v.s: Constantius II orders Julian to be stationed in Paris to continue defending the Rhine frontier. During his time in Paris, Julian's relationship with the Gaulish populace and army grows increasingly close, and the emperor begins to suspect his popularity.

v.t: Former Yan expands south, capturing Eastern Jin territories north of the Huai River
v.s: Taking advantage of the Eastern Jin's failed northern expeditions, Former Yan Emperor Murong Jun sends troops south to seize the Eastern Jin's Huai River region, advancing as far as the Huai River. The Eastern Jin is forced to retreat to the Huainan line, and the north-south confrontation becomes more pronounced.

v.t: Sasanian Persia besieges and captures the Roman fortress of Amida
v.s: Shapur II of Sasanian Persia personally leads a large army to besiege the Roman Mesopotamian stronghold of Amida. The Roman garrison resists tenaciously; the siege lasts 73 days before the city falls, and the Persian army massacres the inhabitants. The Romans suffer heavy losses, crippling their strength.

v.t: Julian is proclaimed emperor by his troops in Gaul, civil war erupts
v.s: Constantius II demands that Julian dispatch elite Gaulish troops east for a campaign against Persia, provoking a mutiny in the Gaulish army. The soldiers, against Julian's wishes, proclaim him Augustus. Julian is forced to accept, and civil war breaks out again in the Roman Empire.

v.t: Former Yan's Murong Jun dies; the young Murong Wei succeeds, with Murong Ke as regent
v.s: Former Yan Emperor Murong Jun dies. His ten-year-old crown prince Murong Wei ascends, with Murong Ke and Murong Ping serving as regents. Murong Ke is lenient and stable, preserving Former Yan's power and maintaining its strength.

v.t: Emperor Mu of Jin dies; Emperor Ai of Jin ascends
v.s: Emperor Mu of Jin dies without issue. The eldest son of Emperor Cheng, Sima Pi, ascends as Emperor Ai of Jin. Emperor Ai is fond of Huang-Lao (Daoist) practices, consumes elixirs, and neglects state affairs; power falls into the hands of Huan Wen.

v.t: Eastern Jin and Former Yan confront each other along the Huai River line
v.s: The Eastern Jin and Former Yan are separated by the Huai River, with small-scale skirmishes continuing along the border. The Eastern Jin strengthens defenses along the Huai, building fortresses, but is unable to launch large-scale northern expeditions.

v.t: Roman Emperor Constantius II dies; Julian becomes sole emperor
v.s: Constantius II dies suddenly while on a campaign against Julian, and on his deathbed recognizes Julian as his successor. Julian enters Constantinople and becomes sole Roman emperor, undertaking religious reforms.

v.t: Roman Emperor Julian launches a pagan revival movement, suppressing Christianity
v.s: Emperor Julian (known as the 'Apostate') attempts to restore Roman traditional polytheism and vigorously suppresses Christianity. He orders the reconstruction of pagan temples, restores sacrifices, forbids Christians from teaching classical literature, and encourages sectarian infighting. However, his reign is short, and he is ultimately unsuccessful.

v.t: Emperor Ai of Jin dies; Emperor Fei of Jin ascends
v.s: Emperor Ai of Jin dies from elixir poisoning. As he has no son, his younger brother, the Prince of Langye Sima Yi, ascends as Emperor Fei of Jin. Huan Wen continues to control the court, and the deposed emperor is a mere puppet.

v.t: Huan Wen monopolizes power, openly intends to depose Emperor Fei of Jin
v.s: Huan Wen exercises total control over the Eastern Jin military and government, commanding elite troops in both the upper and lower Yangtze. He falsely accuses Emperor Fei of Jin of misconduct and pressures Empress Dowager Chu to issue an edict deposing the emperor, preparing to install a new ruler.

v.t: Roman Emperor Julian campaigns against Persia and is killed in battle
v.s: Emperor Julian launches an expedition against the Sasanian Persian Empire, initially making good progress. However, in a battle near Ctesiphon, Julian is overconfident and advances rashly, is wounded by a Persian spear, and dies. On his deathbed, he is said to have acknowledged, 'Galilean (Christian), you have won.'

v.t: Huan Wen implements the 'Gengxu Tuduan,' reorganizing the refugee household registration
v.s: Huan Wen implements the 'Gengxu Tuduan' policy in the Eastern Jin, strictly auditing household registers and incorporating northern refugee populations into local registries, thereby increasing tax revenues. This policy curbs the privileges of the powerful clans in concealing people, but faces strong opposition and is difficult to fully enforce.

v.t: Former Yan captures Luoyang, nullifying Eastern Jin's northern gains
v.s: Taking advantage of internal turmoil in the Eastern Jin, Former Yan sends Murong Ke to capture Luoyang. The defending commander Shen Jin dies in battle, and the results of the Eastern Jin's many years of northern expeditions are wiped out. Thereafter, Luoyang remains in the hands of northern regimes for a long time.

v.t: New Roman Emperor Jovian makes peace with Persia, ceding border territories
v.s: After Julian's death, the Roman army proclaims Jovian emperor. To quickly end the war, Jovian signs a humiliating peace treaty with Shapur II, ceding five provinces in northern Mesopotamia, including the fortress of Nisibis. Rome's eastern defenses are significantly weakened.

v.t: Excavation of the Mogao Caves at Dunhuang begins
v.s: The Former Liang monk Le Zun excavates the first cave on the cliff face opposite the Sanwei Mountains southeast of Dunhuang, for meditation and worship. Thereafter, the Mogao Caves are continuously expanded over a thousand years, becoming the world's largest and longest-lasting treasure house of Buddhist art.

v.t: Valens is appointed Eastern Roman Emperor
v.s: Roman Emperor Valentinian I appoints his brother Valens as Eastern Emperor, responsible for governing the eastern provinces of the empire and resisting Persian and barbarian invasions. Valens adheres to Arian Christianity, exacerbating religious conflict.

v.t: Fu Shuang and others, brothers of Former Qin's Fu Jian, launch a rebellion
v.s: Several brothers of Former Qin Emperor Fu Jian (Fu Shuang, Fu Liu, etc.), opposing Wang Meng's reforms, rise in rebellion. Fu Jian sends Wang Meng to suppress the rebellion, which is quickly quelled, consolidating his rule.

v.t: Western Emperor Valentinian I stabilizes the frontier in the West
v.s: Valentinian I builds forts along the Danube and Rhine, severely punishes barbarian invasions, and pursues a lenient religious policy. His reign is relatively stable; he is one of the last strong Western Roman emperors.

v.t: Picts and Scots jointly invade Roman Britain
v.s: The Picts of northern Britain and the Scots from Ireland join forces to raid the Roman province of Britain, while Roman defenders are overwhelmed. Valentinian I sends his general Theodosius (father of Theodosius the Great) to their aid.

v.t: Wang Meng's reforms are fully implemented; legal construction in Former Qin bears fruit
v.s: Under Wang Meng's direction, Former Qin abolishes the privileges of the old aristocracy, standardizes weights and measures, establishes state schools, and perfects a civil service examination-like system for selecting officials. Former Qin becomes politically clear, its national strength greatly enhanced, and it has the capability to unify the north.

v.t: Huan Wen's third northern expedition: crushing defeat at Fangtou
v.s: Huan Wen leads 50,000 infantry and cavalry north against Former Yan. Initially advancing well, he reaches Fangtou (present-day Xun County, Henan). However, the Former Yan general Murong Chui cuts off the Jin supply lines, and Huan Wen is defeated, losing over 30,000 men. The Fangtou defeat severely damages Huan Wen's prestige.

v.t: Former Yan general Murong Chui is suspected and forced to defect to Former Qin
v.s: Murong Chui, the hero of the Fangtou battle, is suspected and persecuted by the Former Yan regent Murong Ping. Forced to flee, he leads his family and followers to seek refuge with Former Qin. Fu Jian is overjoyed and gives him an important post, hastening the downfall of Former Yan.

v.t: The Roman Empire recovers Britain, suppressing barbarian revolts
v.s: Theodosius leads Roman forces to land in Britain, defeats the Picts and Scots, recaptures lost towns, and rebuilds Hadrian's Wall, restoring Roman rule in Britain.

v.t: Former Qin destroys Former Yan, unifying northern China
v.s: Fu Jian sends Wang Meng and Murong Chui to attack Former Yan. The Qin army routs the main Former Yan force at Luchuan and besieges Yecheng; Murong Wei surrenders, and Former Yan falls. Former Qin unifies northern China (except for Former Liang and Dai), forming a north-south confrontation with the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Remnant Xiongnu tribes migrate westward to the East European steppe
v.s: The descendants of the Northern Xiongnu (known as the Huns), defeated by the Eastern Han, after centuries of migration, reach the North Caucasus and the East European steppe north of the Caspian Sea. They come into contact with the local Alans and Goths, triggering a subsequent wave of barbarian migrations.

v.t: The Roman Temple incident in Jerusalem: conflict between Jews and Christians
v.s: During the reign of Roman Emperor Valens, a conflict erupts between Christians and Jews in Jerusalem. The Roman governor allows Christians to destroy Jewish synagogues and pagan temples, but the incident is eventually resolved without escalating into large-scale persecution.

v.t: Huan Wen deposes Emperor Fei of Jin and establishes Emperor Jianwen of Jin
v.s: Huan Wen leads his troops into the capital, falsely accusing Emperor Fei of impotence and inability to produce an heir, and forces Empress Dowager Chu to depose him. He then installs the emperor's brother, the Prince of Kuaiji Sima Yu, as Emperor Jianwen of Jin. Huan Wen thereby fully controls the Eastern Jin court.

v.t: The Roman Empire and Sasanian Persia vie for control of Armenia
v.s: Roman Emperor Valens and Persian Shapur II face off over the succession to the Armenian throne. They exchange envoys for years of negotiations, but never reach an agreement, leading to frequent border clashes.

v.t: Emperor Jianwen of Jin dies; Emperor Xiaowu ascends; Xie An comes to court as regent
v.s: Emperor Jianwen of Jin dies, and his crown prince Sima Yao ascends as Emperor Xiaowu of Jin. Huan Wen, who had hoped to usurp the throne, is blocked by Xie An, Wang Tanzhi, and others. Xie An is called to court and begins to govern the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Former Qin's Fu Jian strengthens control over the Western Regions, opening the Silk Road
v.s: Fu Jian sends his general Lü Guang on a campaign to the Western Regions, bringing Kucha, Shule, and other states under control. The southern Silk Road is reopened, facilitating trade and Buddhist exchange between East and West, and promoting cultural prosperity in Former Qin.

v.t: Huan Wen of the Eastern Jin dies; Huan Chong voluntarily yields power; Xie An becomes the pillar
v.s: Huan Wen, who had attempted to usurp the throne, dies. His brother Huan Chong succeeds as Inspector of Jingzhou. For the sake of stability, Huan Chong voluntarily yields some of his military command and cooperates with Xie An. Xie An thus becomes the central figure of the Eastern Jin court, uniting all parties to resist Former Qin.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Valens persecutes orthodox Christians
