v.s: Emperor Valens, an adherent of Arianism, begins a large-scale persecution of Nicene orthodox Christians, exiling many bishops and confiscating orthodox churches. This deepens religious divisions within the Eastern Empire.

v.t: Western Emperor Valentinian I dies; Gratian succeeds
v.s: Roman Western Emperor Valentinian I dies of a stroke of anger on the Danube front; his son Gratian succeeds as Western Emperor. Young and capable, Gratian appoints the famous general Theodosius (Theodosius the Great) to manage the Balkans and Danubian defenses.

v.t: Xie An returns from seclusion and formally takes charge of the Eastern Jin government
v.s: After living in seclusion at Dongshan for many years, Xie An, invited by Huan Wen, returns to the court and enters the central government. With his broad-mindedness and leniency, he coordinates Jing and Yang provinces, stabilizing internal unity in the Eastern Jin and laying the foundation for later victory at the Battle of Fei River.

v.t: Former Qin Grand Chancellor Wang Meng dies; Fu Jian loses his pillar
v.s: Wang Meng, the Grand Chancellor of Former Qin and a great minister of the age, dies. On his deathbed, he advises Fu Jian not to attack the Eastern Jin hastily, but first to eliminate internal threats from the Xianbei and Qiang. Fu Jian does not heed his advice, leading to the rapid collapse of Former Qin.

v.t: The Hephthalites (White Huns) begin entering Central Asia, threatening the Sasanian Empire
v.s: The Hephthalite nomadic tribes (White Huns) migrate west from the Altai Mountains into Transoxiana, defeating the remnants of the Kushan Empire there. They begin to threaten the northeastern border of the Sasanian Persian Empire, forcing Shapur II to strengthen his eastern defenses.

v.t: Shapur II of Sasanian Persia dies
v.s: Shapur II, one of the greatest monarchs of the Sasanian Persian Empire (who had fought the Roman Empire for decades), dies. His son Ardashir II succeeds. The momentum of Persian expansion temporarily slows.

v.t: Former Qin destroys Former Liang, unifying the last region of the north
v.s: Fu Jian sends generals Gou Chang and Yao Chang to march west and attack Former Liang, which controlled the Hexi Corridor. The Former Liang ruler Zhang Tianxi surrenders, and Former Liang falls. In the same year, Former Qin destroys the Dai state, completely unifying northern China.

v.t: The Visigoths, driven by the Huns, request to enter the Roman Empire for refuge
v.s: The Huns migrating west defeat and displace the Ostrogoths. The Visigoths, fearing the Huns, gather on the north bank of the Danube and request permission to cross into the Roman Empire for refuge. Eastern Emperor Valens grants their request.

v.t: Xie Xuan of the Eastern Jin forms the 'Beifu Army', training elite troops
v.s: The Eastern Jin Chancellor Xie An orders his nephew Xie Xuan to form a new army at Jingkou (present-day Zhenjiang), recruiting northern refugees and sons of military families. This army, strictly trained and extremely combat-effective, becomes known as the 'Beifu Army' and is the main force in the later Battle of Fei River.

v.t: The Visigoths received into the Roman Empire revolt in Thrace
v.s: Roman officials mistreat the Visigothic refugees, even selling them into slavery. The Visigoths, pushed to the limit, revolt, defeat the local Roman garrison, and begin to plunder and ravage Thrace.

v.t: Fu Jian of Former Qin actively prepares to attack the Eastern Jin
v.s: Ignoring the objections of his ministers, Fu Jian begins large-scale conscription and stockpiling of grain, preparing to attack the Eastern Jin. He forcibly conscripts all males over fifteen into the army, with a total force purportedly numbering one million.

v.t: Battle of Adrianople: Visigothic cavalry routs the Roman army; Valens is killed
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Valens engages the Visigoths at Adrianople (present-day Edirne, Turkey) in a decisive battle. The traditional Roman infantry phalanx is outflanked and crushed by Visigothic heavy cavalry; about 40,000 Roman soldiers are killed, and Valens himself perishes. This battle marks the rise of cavalry as the dominant force on the battlefield, replacing infantry.

v.t: Former Qin captures the Eastern Jin stronghold Xiangyang, opening the gateway to the south
v.s: Former Qin's large army besieges the Eastern Jin city of Xiangyang. The defending commander Zhu Xu is defeated and captured, and Xiangyang falls. Former Qin gains a strategic foothold in the middle Yangtze, paving the way for a large-scale southern campaign.

v.t: Theodosius is appointed Eastern Roman Emperor, beginning the restoration
v.s: After the disastrous defeat at Adrianople, Western Emperor Gratian appoints the military genius Theodosius (son of Theodosius the Elder) as Eastern Emperor. Theodosius quickly reorganizes the army, makes peace with the Goths, and temporarily halts the collapse of the Roman East.

v.t: Teotihuacan influence extends into the Maya region
v.s: The armies of the Teotihuacan civilization from the Mexican highlands enter the Maya lowlands, conquering city-states such as Tikal. Teotihuacan-style architecture, pottery, and murals begin to appear in the Maya region, profoundly transforming Maya culture.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius I stabilizes the situation, concluding a truce with the Goths
v.s: After ascending the throne, Theodosius I, through flexible negotiations and military deterrence, reaches a temporary truce with the Visigoths, allowing them to settle in the Balkans. He is thereby able to turn his attention to internal affairs and religious matters.

v.t: Former Qin continues its southern advance; the Eastern Jin situation becomes critical
v.s: In the 4th year of Taiyuan (379), after capturing Xiangyang, Former Qin's military advance threatens the Yangtze and Huai River region. The Eastern Jin's defense lines are stretched, and the court panics. Under Xie An's command, Xie Xuan intensifies the training of the Beifu Army in preparation for the Battle of Fei River (383). Fu Jian's southern campaign plan is accelerating.

v.t: Theodosius issues the Edict of Thessalonica, making Christianity the state religion of the Roman Empire
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius I, together with his Western co-emperor, issues an edict declaring Nicene Christianity the sole legal religion of the Roman Empire, and forbidding pagan worship and Arianism. From this point on, Christianity becomes the state church of the empire.

v.t: The Beifu Army shows its prowess, repeatedly defeating Former Qin reconnaissance forces along the border
v.s: The Beifu Army led by Xie Xuan launches harassing operations along the Huai River, repeatedly defeating Former Qin scouting and advance units. The formidable fighting power of the Beifu Army astonishes the Former Qin court.

v.t: Samudragupta of the Gupta Empire campaigns south, conquering the kingdoms of the Deccan
v.s: Gupta Emperor Samudragupta (Sea Protector) leads an army southward, defeating over ten kingdoms in the Deccan Plateau through a series of wars, forcing them to pay tribute. The Gupta Empire reaches its greatest territorial extent.

v.t: Hinduism experiences a revival under the patronage of the Gupta Empire
v.s: The Gupta Empire, favoring Hinduism, actively sponsors temple construction and Brahminic rituals. The scriptures of Vaishnavism and Shaivism (such as the Puranas) are codified during this period, and Hinduism overtakes Buddhism as the dominant religion in central and northern India.

v.t: The First Council of Constantinople convenes, reaffirming the Nicene Creed
v.s: Theodosius I convenes the Second Ecumenical Council in Constantinople, which formally reaffirms the authority of the Nicene Creed and condemns Arianism, Macedonianism, and other heresies. The council also elevates the see of Constantinople to a position second only to Rome.

v.t: Fu Jian, ignoring opposition, fully prepares for a southern campaign against the Eastern Jin
v.s: Fu Jian ignores the remonstrances of his crown prince Fu Hong and ministers like Fu Rong, and insists on launching a large-scale war against the Eastern Jin. He conscripts one million men nationwide and advances by both land and water, intending to annihilate the Eastern Jin in one fell swoop.

v.t: The Former Qin army is fully assembled; Fu Jian vows to march south
v.s: Fu Jian vows an oath at Chang'an; his million-strong army marches south. The vanguard, Fu Rong, leads 300,000 infantry and cavalry, with the main force following. Fu Jian arrogantly declares, 'By throwing our whips into the Yangtze, we can stop its flow.' The Eastern Jin court and people are terrified.

v.t: Xie An, unflappable, calmly deploys the Eastern Jin defense line
v.s: Faced with the overwhelming Former Qin army, Xie An remains calm in the midst of chaos. He appoints Xie Shi as Grand Marshal of the Expedition, Xie Xuan as Vanguard Commander, leading 80,000 Beifu troops to meet the enemy. He himself remains behind, playing chess with a guest, to stabilize public morale.

v.t: The Roman Empire concludes a peace treaty with the Visigoths; Theodosius uses barbarians to fight barbarians
v.s: Theodosius I signs a peace treaty with the Visigothic chieftain, allowing them to settle in the Balkans and provide troops. Many Visigoths enter the Roman army, and Theodosius uses them to strengthen his forces against Western usurpers.

v.t: Battle of Fei River: The Eastern Jin decisively defeats Former Qin, shattering Fu Jian's dream of unification
v.s: The Eastern Jin Beifu Army engages the million-strong Former Qin army at the Fei River (present-day Shou County, Anhui). The Jin army takes advantage of a flaw in the Former Qin deployment, crossing the river to attack, while the Former Qin defector Zhu Xu shouts from the rear, 'The Qin army is defeated!' The Former Qin army breaks into a complete rout. Fu Jian is severely wounded and flees north in disgrace.

v.t: Xie An remains composed upon receiving the victory report, though inwardly overjoyed
v.s: When the victory report of the Battle of Fei River reaches Jiankang, Xie An is playing chess with a guest. He glances at the dispatch and continues playing. When asked, he calmly replies, 'The children have defeated the enemy.' After the guest leaves, he is so excited that the teeth of his clogs break.

v.t: Western Roman Emperor Gratian is assassinated; the empire is thrown into turmoil
v.s: While on campaign in Britain, Gratian is assassinated by a rebel general. The Spanish general Magnus Maximus proclaims himself Western Emperor, and Theodosius I is forced to recognize his status.

v.t: Murong Chui rebels against Former Qin and establishes the Later Yan regime
v.s: Taking advantage of the chaos after the Battle of Fei River and the collapse of the north, Murong Chui, a general of Former Qin, proclaims himself King of Yan at Xingyang, establishing the Later Yan regime. He quickly rallies his old Xianbei followers, captures Yecheng, and restores the Yan state.

v.t: Western Roman usurper Maximus formally proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Maximus controls Gaul, Britain, and Spain, with formidable power. He demands formal recognition from Eastern Emperor Theodosius I, who, to stabilize the situation, temporarily recognizes him as Western Emperor.

v.t: Yao Chang strangles Fu Jian; the Former Qin emperor dies
v.s: Former Qin Emperor Fu Jian flees to Mount Wujiang, where he is captured by the Qiang general Yao Chang. Yao Chang demands the imperial seal; Fu Jian refuses and is strangled. After Fu Jian's death, Former Qin exists in name only, and the north descends into renewed fragmentation.

v.t: Tuoba Gui re-establishes the Dai state at Niuchuan, the prototype of Northern Wei
v.s: The Tuoba Xianbei chieftain Tuoba Gui convenes a tribal assembly at Niuchuan (present-day Inner Mongolia), re-establishing the Dai state and proclaiming himself King of Dai. Soon he changes the state name to Wei, known as Northern Wei, laying the foundation for later unification of the north.

v.t: Qifu Guoren establishes the Western Qin regime
v.s: Taking advantage of Former Qin's collapse, the Longxi Xianbei chieftain Qifu Guoren proclaims himself King of Qin at Yuanchuan (present-day Yuzhong, Gansu), establishing the Western Qin regime. Western Qin controls Longxi, coexisting with Later Qin and the remnants of Former Qin.

v.t: Theodosius I further strengthens the position of Orthodox Christianity
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius I issues a series of decrees, closing pagan temples and banning pagan activities such as the Olympic Games. Christianity becomes the sole legal religion of the empire, profoundly influencing medieval European civilization.

v.t: Lü Guang returns from the Western Regions and establishes the Later Liang regime
v.s: The Former Qin general Lü Guang, returning from his campaign in the Western Regions and learning of Fu Jian's death, proclaims himself Governor of Liangzhou, establishing the Later Liang regime. Later Liang controls the Hexi Corridor, becoming an important power in the northwest.

v.t: Tuoba Gui changes his title to King of Wei, establishing the Northern Wei state name
v.s: Tuoba Gui abandons the title of King of Dai and changes it to King of Wei, formally adopting 'Wei' as the state name. He recruits Han gentry, establishes government institutions, and lays the foundation for the later founding of the capital.

v.t: Murong Chui of Later Yan consolidates the north, annexing most of Hebei
v.s: Later Yan Emperor Murong Chui leads his army to capture Huatai and Luoyang, heavily defeating the Eastern Jin defenders, and gains complete control of the area north of the Yellow River. Later Yan becomes one of the most powerful states in the north.

v.t: Eastern Emperor Theodosius I defeats the Western usurper Maximus
v.s: Theodosius I leads his army west, defeating the usurper Maximus's forces in northern Italy. Maximus is captured and executed, and Theodosius reunifies the western part of the Roman Empire, becoming the sole emperor.

v.t: Lü Guang of Later Liang formally proclaims himself king
v.s: Lü Guang proclaims himself King of Sanhe at Guzang, establishing an independent kingdom. He is a devout Buddhist, inviting the Western Regions monk Kumarajiva to translate scriptures in Liangzhou, which becomes a major Buddhist center.

v.t: The Roman Empire is briefly unified under Theodosius I
v.s: After defeating Maximus, Theodosius I brings the West under his rule, achieving the final brief unification of the Roman Empire. He appoints his young son Honorius as Western co-emperor while retaining overall control.

v.t: The Serapeum of Alexandria is destroyed by Christians
v.s: With the support of Theodosius I, Theophilus, the bishop of Alexandria, leads a Christian mob to destroy the famous Serapeum temple in the city, marking the destruction of the last academic center of paganism.

v.t: Northern Wei's Tuoba Gui defeats the Xiongnu Tiefu tribe, unifying the Daibei region
v.s: Tuoba Gui of Northern Wei leads his army to attack the Xiongnu Tiefu tribe of Liu Weichen in the Hetao region, achieving a great victory; Liu Weichen is killed. Tuoba Gui absorbs his followers, expanding his territory to the west bank of the Yellow River.

v.t: The Prince of Kuaiji, Sima Daozi, monopolizes power in the Eastern Jin, leading to political chaos
v.s: After the Battle of Fei River, Xie An dies, and the Prince of Kuaiji, Sima Daozi, becomes Grand Chancellor. He is greedy and inept, employing petty men and excluding worthy ministers. He competes for power with the emperor's younger brother Sima Yuanxian, leading to increasing political corruption in the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Thessalonica massacre: Roman army suppresses a civilian revolt
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius I, enraged by the killing of his garrison commander by the people of Thessalonica, orders a reprisal. Gothic troops enter the city's hippodrome and massacre about 7,000 civilians. This act shocks Ambrose, the bishop of Milan, who forces Theodosius to do penance.

v.t: Theodosius I completely bans pagan activities
v.s: Theodosius I issues an edict prohibiting all pagan sacrifices, divination, and worship, including in private spaces. All pagan temples are either closed or converted into churches. Christianity becomes the sole legal religion of the empire.

v.t: Yao Xing of Later Qin succeeds and reorganizes internal affairs
v.s: Yao Chang, founding emperor of Later Qin, dies, and his crown prince Yao Xing succeeds. Yao Xing respects Confucianism, establishes schools, and employs Han gentry; the state power of Later Qin gradually strengthens, making it an important regime in the Guanzhong region.

v.t: Northern Wei defeats the remnants of the Tiefu Xiongnu, completely controlling the Hetao region
v.s: Tuoba Gui of Northern Wei again campaigns west, completely eliminating the remnants of the Tiefu Xiongnu and occupying the Hetao region. Northern Wei's territory expands to the middle and upper Yellow River, surpassing Later Yan in power.

v.t: Murong Chui of Later Yan destroys the Zhai Wei, expanding his territory
v.s: In 392 CE, Later Yan Emperor Murong Chui leads his army to attack and destroy the Zhai Wei regime, which occupied Henan, annexing its territory. Zhai Wei was founded by the Dingling people under Zhai Liao. Later Yan's territory reaches its peak, becoming one of the most powerful states in the north. Murong Chui is the most outstanding military commander of the Sixteen Kingdoms period.

v.t: Western usurper Eugenius proclaims himself emperor in the West, challenging Theodosius
v.s: The Western Roman general Eugenius is proclaimed Western Emperor by the Frankish general Arbogast. Gaining the support of the Senate, he attempts to revive paganism and breaks with Theodosius I.

v.t: Murong Chui of Later Yan destroys Western Yan, unifying the eastern part of the passes
v.s: Later Yan Emperor Murong Chui personally campaigns against Western Yan, defeating them at Taibi and advancing to capture Zhangzi. Western Yan falls; Later Yan incorporates its followers, unifying the vast regions of Shandong, Hebei, and Shanxi.

v.t: Theodosius I holds the last ancient Olympic Games
v.s: Theodosius I orders the holding of the last ancient Olympic Games at Olympia. He then issues an edict banning the Olympics as a pagan activity, ending a thousand-year tradition.

v.t: Battle of the Frigidus: Theodosius I defeats Eugenius, finally reuniting Rome
v.s: Theodosius I engages the Western usurper Eugenius at the Frigidus River (in present-day Slovenia). Relying on the charge of his Gothic mercenaries, Theodosius wins a great victory. Eugenius is killed, and the Roman Empire is united for the last time.

v.t: Yao Xing of Later Qin captures Luoyang from the Eastern Jin
v.s: Taking advantage of the Eastern Jin's internal strife, Yao Xing of Later Qin sends troops to capture Luoyang, taking the Eastern Jin defenders captive. Luoyang again falls into the hands of a northern regime, and the Eastern Jin's northern gains are lost once more.

v.t: Lü Guang of Later Liang kills officials; his rule becomes increasingly tyrannical
v.s: In 394 CE, Lü Guang, the ruler of Later Liang, becomes suspicious of his ministers and imperial clan members, executing several who had remonstrated directly. Internal conflicts in Later Liang intensify, and its power begins to decline. After Lü Guang's death, Later Liang rapidly splits and is partitioned by Northern Liang and Southern Liang.

v.t: The Roman Empire permanently splits into Eastern and Western halves
v.s: Roman Emperor Theodosius I dies in Milan, dividing the empire between his two sons: Arcadius receives the Eastern Roman Empire (capital Constantinople), and Honorius receives the Western Roman Empire (capital Ravenna). Thereafter, the two halves are never reunited; the Western Roman Empire falls in 476 CE, while the Eastern Roman Empire continues until 1453 CE.

v.t: Later Yan suffers a devastating defeat at the Battle of Canhe Slope by Northern Wei, losing its elite forces
v.s: Murong Bao, crown prince of Later Yan, leads 80,000 elite troops to attack Northern Wei. Tuoba Gui lures the enemy deep and sets an ambush at Canhe Slope (present-day Liangcheng, Inner Mongolia). The Later Yan army is routed, and tens of thousands are captured and massacred. Later Yan never recovers.

v.t: Emperor Xiaowu of Jin is killed by his concubine Lady Zhang; Sima Daozi and his brother usurp power
v.s: Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, joking while drunk that he would depose Lady Zhang, is killed by her in collusion with eunuchs while he sleeps. The death is reported as a nightmare. After his death, his younger brother Sima Daozi and Sima Yuanxian monopolize power, and the Eastern Jin court becomes even more chaotic.

v.t: Visigothic leader Alaric invades the Greek peninsula
v.s: Taking advantage of the chaos in the Roman Empire, Visigothic King Alaric leads his army south into Greece. He sacks the port of Piraeus and threatens Athens, but dares not occupy the historic city. The Eastern Roman Empire is forced to make peace, ceding the province of Illyria.

v.t: Tuoba Gui of Northern Wei formally proclaims himself emperor, establishing the Northern Wei dynasty
v.s: Tuoba Gui of Northern Wei formally ascends the imperial throne at Shengle, known as Emperor Daowu, with the state name Wei. Modeling his government on Han institutions, he establishes a bureaucracy and formulates laws, transforming Northern Wei from a tribal confederation into a centralized feudal dynasty.

v.t: Murong Chui, founding emperor of Later Yan, dies; Later Yan's power declines
v.s: Later Yan Emperor Murong Chui dies of illness while leading an army against Northern Wei. His son Murong Bao succeeds, but after the devastating defeat at Canhe Slope, Later Yan's elite forces are lost, its power declines rapidly, and it quickly moves toward fragmentation.

v.t: Western Roman general Stilicho defeats the Visigoth Alaric
v.s: Stilicho, commander of the Western Roman army, leads his forces into Greece and successfully defeats the Visigothic king Alaric, forcing him to retreat to Illyria. Stilicho intends to destroy the Visigoths entirely, but is prevented by Eastern Roman interference.

v.t: Tufa Wugu establishes the Southern Liang regime
v.s: The Hexi Xianbei chieftain Tufa Wugu proclaims himself King of Xiping at Lianchuan (present-day Ledu, Qinghai), establishing the Southern Liang regime. Southern Liang competes with Later Liang and Northern Liang for control of the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: Duan Ye establishes the Northern Liang regime
v.s: Duan Ye, Grand Administrator of Jiankang of Later Liang, is supported by Juqu Mengxun and others to become Governor of Liangzhou, breaking away from Later Liang to establish an independent state, known as Northern Liang. Northern Liang is later usurped by Juqu Mengxun and becomes one of the Sixteen Kingdoms.

v.t: Stilicho campaigns in North Africa, suppressing a local rebellion
v.s: Stilicho, commander of the Western Roman army, leads his forces to North Africa to suppress a rebellion against Roman rule. He successfully restores order, ensuring the supply of grain from North Africa to the city of Rome, temporarily stabilizing the economy of the Western Empire.

v.t: Northern Wei formally moves its capital to Pingcheng, building palaces
v.s: Tuoba Gui, Emperor Daowu of Northern Wei, moves the capital from Shengle to Pingcheng (present-day Datong, Shanxi), and begins large-scale construction of palaces, ancestral temples, and altars. Pingcheng becomes the political, economic, and cultural center of Northern Wei.

v.t: Murong De establishes the Southern Yan regime
v.s: Murong De, a member of the Later Yan imperial clan, proclaims himself King of Yan at Huatai, establishing the Southern Yan regime, and later moves the capital to Guanggu (present-day Qingzhou, Shandong). Southern Yan controls the Shandong Peninsula, coexisting with Later Yan, Northern Wei, the Eastern Jin, and other regimes.

v.t: The Sun En rebellion breaks out, causing great chaos in the southeastern Eastern Jin
v.s: Sun En of Langye, a follower of the Way of the Five Pecks of Rice, after his uncle Sun Tai is executed by the Prince of Kuaiji Sima Daozi, flees to an island. He gathers followers and rebels, capturing Shangyu, Kuaiji, and other places. The common people flock to him, and within ten days he commands hundreds of thousands.

v.t: John Chrysostom, Patriarch of Constantinople, begins to lead the church
v.s: The famous church orator John (Golden Mouth) is appointed Patriarch of Constantinople. He strongly criticizes court luxury and corruption and implements church reforms, winning the support of the people but incurring the enmity of Empress Eudoxia.

v.t: Faxian departs from Chang'an for the west in search of Buddhist scriptures, heading to India
v.s: The Eastern Jin monk Faxian, lamenting the lack of complete monastic rules in China, departs from Chang'an with companions, traversing the Hexi Corridor, the Western Regions, and crossing the Pamirs to India. After over ten years, he returns with several important Vinaya texts and writes the 'Record of Buddhistic Kingdoms'.

v.t: The Western Regions monk Kumarajiva is welcomed to Chang'an by Later Qin to translate scriptures
v.s: Yao Xing, emperor of Later Qin, attacks Liangzhou and welcomes the monk Kumarajiva to Chang'an, treating him as a state preceptor. Kumarajiva organizes a large-scale translation center in Chang'an, translating dozens of Buddhist scriptures including the Diamond Sutra and the Lotus Sutra, laying the foundation for Chinese Buddhist canon.

v.t: The Sun En rebel army captures Kuaiji and approaches Jiankang
v.s: Sun En leads the rebel army to capture the commandery seat of Kuaiji, killing the Grand Administrator Wang Ningzhi. The Eastern Jin court is alarmed, sending Xie Yan, Liu Laozhi, and others with the Beifu Army to suppress the rebellion. Sun En moves through Wu, at one point approaching Jiankang, with tremendous momentum.

v.t: The Donatist schism in North Africa becomes severe
v.s: Within North African Christianity, a serious split arises over the issue of ordination during the Great Persecution. The Donatist faction insists that sacraments performed by traditores (those who had handed over scriptures) are invalid, forming a schism from the orthodox church. This sect has great influence in North Africa for over a hundred years.

v.t: Eastern Jin general Liu Yu distinguishes himself in the Sun En rebellion
v.s: Liu Yu, a general of the Beifu Army, fighting against Sun En, leads from the front and repeatedly performs outstanding feats. He defeats Sun En at Juzhang, Haiyan, and other places, forcing Sun En to retreat to his island base. Liu Yu's military talent gains widespread recognition.

v.t: Li Gao establishes the Western Liang regime
v.s: Li Gao, Grand Administrator of Dunhuang of Northern Liang (a Han Chinese), feeling threatened by Juqu Mengxun, proclaims himself Duke of Liang at Dunhuang, establishing the Western Liang regime. Western Liang respects Confucianism, protects its territory and people, and creates a brief period of cultural prosperity in the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: The Gainas rebellion breaks out in the Eastern Roman Empire; a barbarian general rebels
v.s: Gainas, a Gothic general in the Eastern Roman army, rebels and forces Emperor Arcadius to make concessions. The people of Constantinople rise up, slaughtering Goths in the city. Gainas flees and is eventually killed by the Hun king Uldin. The Eastern Roman Empire temporarily frees itself from barbarian control.

v.t: Easter Island begins carving giant stone statues (Moai)
v.s: On Easter Island in the South Pacific, the Polynesian inhabitants begin carving giant stone statues (Moai) from volcanic tuff. These statues are believed to represent deified ancestors. Archaeological evidence shows that the earliest carving activity began around 400 CE.
